input_candidates.truth_value: Figure 1: Number of publications by research topic (lightweight steel and Fe–Mn–Al–C steel) from 2010 to 2022 according to Scopus bibliometric database.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Figure 1 (publication trends 2010-2022), Figure 3 (Fe-20Mn-Al-C phase diagrams at 900°C and 1200°C), and Figure 4 (mechanical properties versus Mn content) are referenced, but detailed numerical values depend on graphical curves not available in the text pipeline.

input_candidates.truth_value: Figure 2: Flowchart comparing multiphase (Mn: 5-12 wt%, C ≤0.8 wt%) and austenitic (Mn >12 wt%, C 0.8-1.2 wt%) Fe–Mn–Al–C steels; multiphase strength 800-1200 MPa, elongation 30-60%; austenitic strength 600-1500 MPa, elongation <60%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Stacking fault energy (SFE) ranges for deformation mechanisms are discussed as general thresholds: TRIP below 12 mJ·m⁻², TWIP at 12-35 mJ·m⁻², and MBIP/slip at 60-100 mJ·m⁻², applicable across multiple alloy systems rather than a single sample.

input_candidates.truth_value: Figure 4: Mechanical properties of lightweight steels with average and high Mn content at room temperature (reproduced from ref.[8]).
input_candidates.prediction_value: For Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-0.5Mo-0.9Si armor steel, a rolled-and-aged plate (538°C, 10 h) reached 420 HB, while a heat-treated (quenched + aged) plate reached approximately 360 HB under similar aging.

input_candidates.truth_value: General classification: Fe–Mn–Al–C steels are divided into ferritic, duplex, and austenitic based on Mn and C content. Austenitic steels with 12–30 wt% Mn, 5–12 wt% Al, 0.6–1.2 wt% C exhibit TWIP/TRIP/MBIP mechanisms.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Addition of 1.0 mass% V to Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C steel reportedly achieved tensile strength of 1300 MPa and specific strength of 190 MPa·cm³·g⁻¹ after cold rolling (20%), annealing at 900°C, and aging at 450-550°C.

input_candidates.truth_value: SFE ranges: <12 mJ/m² → TRIP; 12–35 mJ/m² → TWIP; 60–100 mJ/m² → MBIP. Al increases SFE, promoting slip and microband-induced plasticity.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Aluminum additions (1-2 wt%) enhance resistance to hydrogen-induced embrittlement in high-Mn TWIP steels by increasing SFE and reducing hydrogen diffusivity.

input_candidates.truth_value: κ-carbide precipitation: intragranular κ’-carbides form by spinodal decomposition (coherent, 2–3 nm), intergranular κ-carbides form by eutectoid reaction at grain boundaries, detrimental to ductility.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Manufacturing challenges highlighted include phosphorus sensitivity, melt-refractory reactions, alumina nozzle clogging during continuous casting, and red scale defects when silicon exceeds 1-2 mass%.

input_candidates.truth_value: Table 1 summarizes the effects of alloying and microalloying elements (Si, Cr, Cu, V, Nb, Mo, Ni) on κ-carbide behavior, microstructure, and mechanical properties.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Aging of Fe-28Mn-9Al-1C steel with 10-12 wt% Al at approximately 550°C can yield approximately 1.15 GPa with approximately 50% elongation due to early formation of coherent nanoscale κ-carbides.

input_candidates.truth_value: Additive manufacturing: Laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) of Fe–Mn–Al–C alloys yields tensile strength >900 MPa and elongation >20%, suitable for complex shaping.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Combined Ti-Mo-Nb addition in Fe-26Mn-8Al-1.5C steel increases yield strength via grain refinement and fine (Ti,Mo,Nb)C particles, but reduces ductility due to coarse κ-carbides and intergranular carbides.

input_candidates.truth_value: Casting challenges: high Mn and Al cause refractory reactions, alumina clogging, and surface defects; new casting schemes needed.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fe-30Mn-9Al-1.2C steel with 2 mass% Si exhibited strain hardening behavior increasing to 83% (relative measure from 20% at 0Si), but this metric is not a standard strain-hardening rate and is retained here as qualitative context.

input_candidates.truth_value: Hydrogen embrittlement: Al additions mitigate hydrogen embrittlement in austenitic Fe–Mn–C steels.
input_candidates.prediction_value: The review notes that lightweight Fe-Mn-Al-C steels are approximately 18% lighter than conventional steels, with reported property ranges of 400-1000 MPa yield strength, 600-2000 MPa ultimate tensile strength, and 30-100% total elongation across the alloy class.
